Detailed information about U-2256’s appearance, theorised evolutionary ancestry, biology, diet, behaviour, vocalisations, life cycle, intelligence, ecological role and cultural significance should be stored in hard copy in vault 1053C at the UO Primary Archive Facility at Black River. The rate of corrosion in both data sources should be monitored carefully, although at present no technique is known for halting or reversing such corrosion.
Although these antimemetic effects linger and rate ω categorisation, U-2256 itself is extinct and does not require a containment protocol.

Description: U-2256 (Cryptomorpha gigantes) is a species of gigafauna that was endemic to the South Pacific Ocean around the islands of Polynesia. U-2256 was one of the very few recorded species known to have developed rudimentary perceptual/ ‘antimemetic’ camouflage, rendering them nearly impossible for other sentient beings to perceive or remember. This adaptation is theorised to have arisen in order to elude predators.

U-2256 was the largest species to ever live on Earth. Resembling spindly, vertically elongated giraffes or brachiosauruses, adults of the species grew to over 1,000 metres in height. They weighed no more than 4 tons, with most of their mass being ‘camouflaged’ by a very similar adaptation. With their broad, dish-shaped feet, they were able to walk directly on the surface of the ocean without sinking.

U-2256 navigated the ocean alone or in X​X​X​X​X​X​X of 2 to as many as 2,000 individuals. They were reluctant to approach land, especially inhabited islands, usually staying more than 30 kilometres offshore.

U-2256 was noted for its macroscopic symbiont gut fauna, U-2256-E (Venteuthis monopsii), sometimes incorrectly referred to by Organisation staff as ‘germs.’ These formed complex societies inside U-2256’s extensive digestive biome, and possessed significant cognitive ability (see attached documents).

Acquisition: Polynesian natives of the island of Takaluʻi used a substance called kāketou for recreational and religious purposes. As well as being a moderate psychedelic, this drug had mnestic properties, suppressing antimemetic effects and making entities camouflaged in this way easier to see and remember. Thus, the Takaluʻians were for millennia the only people able to see U-2256. In Takaluʻian mythology U-2256 were wandering spirits whom the gods had charged with maintaining the horizon, to ensure that the sky and the water never mixed. They were characterised as well-meaning and friendly, but unintelligent and often deficient in their duties, resulting in storms and typhoons. They were called lopoʻongakau, ‘the ones who walk very slowly.’
Takaluʻi was colonised by the British in the early 19th century. By 1890 British missionaries had converted most of the islanders to Protestantism.

In 1991 an Organisation-internal biochemistry study noted that the traditional kāketou substance bore a strong chemical resemblance to the Organisation’s own Class W mnestic. An Organisation anthropologist was assigned to follow X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ the Takaluʻian legend, and became the first outsider to observe X​-2256. An observation X​X​X​X​X​X​ was quickly X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ on the island to study the creatures. Routine containment analysis found that U-2256 fell in Category 1 and required no containment protocol, or even particular X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ secrecy.

History: U-X​X​X​X​ immediately proved to be impossible to capture photographically. Photographic negatives of the species faded into transparency over the X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ a few minutes. Similar decay X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ affected videotape, audio tape, celluloid film, digital and electronic scans etc. The observation team soon returned most of their equipment to inventory and proceeded using pencils and X​X​X​X​X​. At the time, it X​X​X​ believed that such recordings would be effectively permanent.

U-2256’s population declined slightly in 1992 and 1993, then dropped sharply from 1994 onward. A combination of contributing factors were observed: illness, infertility and an X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ rate of stillbirths.

In 2002 a field generator was developed that could penetrate and neutralise U-2256’s antimemetic X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​, allowing for conventional photography. The first and only close-up photograph of one of the creatures instantly killed it. It was concluded that direct observation of U-2256 is injurious to X​X​X​X​. This adaptation is believed to have arisen as a means of detecting predators, just as U-2256’s antimemetic camouflage protected them from those same predators. Use of X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ generator was immediately curtailed.

It was subsequently hypothesised that the Organisation’s ongoing passive observation of the species was intense enough to have harmful effects on U-2256, and that X​X​X​X​ was what was driving the species X​X​X​X​ extinction. Opinions differed sharply X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ veracity of this hypothesis, over X​X​X​ thoroughly it should be tested, and over what X​X​X​X​X​X​ be done if it proved to be true. Several extreme options were X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​, including X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ exterminating X​-X​X​X​X​ to preserve the data, and completely expunging the data to preserve X​-2256. No firm conclusions X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​.

In 2003 observation of U-2256 was scaled back significantly, and the Organisation X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ focus from gathering X​X​X​ data to analysing X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ data. However, U-2256’s population continued to X​X​X​X​X​X​X​. The last individual died near Tokelau X​X​ October 30, 2006.

In X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ discovered X​X​X​X​ the antimemetic camouflage X​X​X​X​X​X​, also characterised as ‘decay’ or ‘corrosion’, was spreading through paper records of X​-X​X​X​X​. As of X​X​X​X​, more X​X​X​X​ 60% of X​X​X​X​X​ documents are X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​, even with X​ strong mnestic dose. The effect is even X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ Unknown entry itself, despite X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ shielding and redundancy in X​X​X​X​ system.
Since X​-X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ extinct, X​X​ new data X​X​X​X​X​ it can be generated. It is X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ that full contamination X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ three to eight years.

chapters.title: 1: Immemorial
paragraphs: Immemorial
‘Ms Quinn! Ms Quinn.’

Marie Quinn has just finished an engineering interlock meeting and is headed back toward her office for a cigarette. Someone is running up to meet her in the corridor. Quinn recognises her as Dr Oli Morgan, a trainee field researcher who joined the Antimemetics Division only six months ago. Part of Simon Lee’s new brood.
‘Dr Morgan. Can I help you with something?’

‘Uhm.’ Morgan interlocks her fingers nervously. She is a full head taller than Quinn and half her age, with an athletic build, incorrigibly curly hair and exceedingly thick glasses. She lacks experience. But she is very smart, and she is learning very fast. In another year, she’ll be among the best people the Division has, or has ever had, and Quinn is looking forward to that. Quinn loves nothing more than competent people.
Still, as the pause lengthens, that day of competence seems to be very much in the future. ‘Dr Morgan, I normally expect my people to get to the point a little quicker than this.’
‘There’s – a stone in the forest behind the site,’ Morgan blurts. ‘It’s monumental. It’s like a skyscraper, it blots out the Sun. Do you know what I’m talking about?’
‘Yes.’
‘But I’ve never seen it before. I don’t understand how it’s possible that I never saw it. It casts a shadow across the whole site. I mean – was it always there?’
‘Yes.’
‘Is this because –’
‘You took your first routine dose of ops-grade mnestics this morning, yes.’
Morgan seems alarmed. ‘That’s how it works? Something that big can just be right there and we don’t see it?’

‘Yeah.’ Quinn checks her watch, and mentally moves some scheduled commitments around. Extend this ‘smoke break’ to the rest of the afternoon. Leave my weekly inspection of U-2200 where it is. Review promotion cases after the gym instead of before. Evening meal … at this rate, never …
Morgan, suffocating under the weight of follow-up questions, finally asks, ‘What is it?’
Quinn gestures to her left, down the corridor, indicating that she is about to walk, and that Morgan should follow her. ‘I’ll show you.’
In the database it’s U-9429. Morgan hasn’t read the entry; she doesn’t have access.

The stone is a single, unbroken, 91-by-91-by-147-metre vertical cuboid of ancient, weathered, dark basalt. It sits at a very slight angle, leaning fractionally to the north. Its regular angles clearly mark it as a carved object, a human-made artifact. It rises out of the forest to the east of UO Wyeleigh and dominates, not to say obliterates, the views in that direction from the windows of the site’s main block. It is, by volume, massively bigger than the site itself, even including its underground extents. It looms. It is absolutely unmissable. The idea that anyone could fail to notice it for any period of time is, Quinn has to admit, more than a little unnerving.

Quinn leads Morgan up the short forest track to the stone’s perimetre, and then right, following its perimetre, in its shadow. It is a wet autumn day, and rain is dripping from the very top edge of the monolith as well as from the conifers that grow right up beside it. The rain makes a constant white hiss, deadening other sounds.

‘There’s a weak antimemetic clouding effect surrounding it,’ Quinn explains as she picks her way along the track ahead of Morgan. ‘To most people it’s effectively invisible. You’ve hiked up to the top of some of these other hills, I’m sure. You should have seen it clearly from up there as well, but you looked straight past it. That’s normal. There’s a related effect that removes people’s memories after they’ve visited the stone. That effect is much stronger. It’ll cut right through your mnestic drug regimen, and mine.’
‘So we’ll forget all about this?’ Morgan asks.

Quinn holds up a battered little notebook and a severely chewed blue ballpoint. Morgan understands; she is carrying a notebook and pen as well. Information suppression is a complicated spectrum. Sometimes a written note is the only thing that will make it out of a zone that suppresses memories, electronic data, radio signals and even audible sound. Alongside the mandatory Organisation-issue ‘brickphone,’ many Antimemetics Division operatives habitually carry some combination of an instant camera, a mechanical tape-driven dictation machine, a notebook, a walkie-talkie …
Not that Morgan was expecting to need anything today.

‘Of course,’ Quinn continues, ‘one side effect of the clouding is that I don’t exactly remember the way. I suppose we could set up signposts, but somehow it never gets done … Not because of antimemetic effects, you understand, just plain laziness … Ah, this looks like the way up.’

They come to a passage in the side of the stone. In fact it is not a passage but a tremendously deep groove, cut all the way from the top of the monolith to its base, a slot with a thin line of overcast sky visible overhead and steps leading up.

At the bottom of the steps, Quinn turns aside and mutters some words. It’s not clear to Morgan what she is saying, or to whom she is saying them. Morgan doesn’t know that she has a follower. But U-4987 cannot enter the area of effect of the stone. It won’t disconnect from her, not that easily, but it cannot follow Quinn up the steps. So, it needs a little reassurance.

Then Quinn begins to climb, and Morgan follows. They climb in silence for some minutes. Morgan stops a few times to write down a note or two, hunching over to shield her notebook from the drizzle. Then she hurries to catch up with Quinn, who maintains a steady, indifferent pace.
Sometime after Morgan has lost count of the steps, the groove makes a ninety-degree turn to the left and continues to ascend. Quinn stops here, above Morgan, and turns to quiz her.
‘What do you have so far?’
‘What is this place?’ Morgan asks.
‘You tell me.’

‘Uhm.’ Morgan hesitates for a moment, uncertain where this is going. She checks her notes. ‘Uhm, well. Geologically speaking, this stone is an alien. At first I thought there could have been a mountain on this spot that was excavated into this shape by human hands. But the rock itself is wrong. It’s different from the mountains and hills near here. Which means it must have been excavated elsewhere, maybe carved there, and moved here.’
Quinn says nothing, but her demeanour seems to indicate that Morgan is on the right track.

‘Which isn’t possible,’ Morgan continues. ‘This is a single stone. Judging from its dimensions and density, it must mass north of three million tons. And that can’t be done. Human civilisation can’t move objects of this size. Not in a single piece. The technology doesn’t exist.’
‘Correct.’
‘So how did it get here?’
‘Good question.’
Morgan doesn’t have the answer to the question. She waits for Quinn to supply it.
But Quinn does not. ‘What else?’

‘… It’s been engraved,’ Morgan says, indicating the walls of the stepped passage. ‘Using tools. And I noticed the exterior walls are the same. There’s a lot of weathering, but here and there between the biological crud there’s this very clear, regular pattern. Right here, see? Tiny vertical rectangles. Like a … block cursor on an old computer terminal.’
‘Or a tombstone in typography,’ Quinn suggests.

Morgan blinks. ‘… Yes. It’s a uniform pattern. Very detailed work, which would require good tools even by modern standards. I think this pattern is supposed to cover the entire exterior of the stone. And if that’s the case, the blocks are so minuscule and the stone is so large that there must have originally been millions of them.’
‘Correct,’ Quinn says again. ‘Anything else?’

Morgan thinks for a minute. She stares up into the rain, reflecting on the atmosphere that the stone, or sculpture as she supposes it would be better described, projects. Loneliness, quiet, desolation, awe … intimidation. And some fear. Although, with that intimidating, fearful atmosphere, there’s no sensation of danger. No threat.
‘“We considered ourselves to be a powerful culture,”’ she says aloud.
Quinn hears this, but asks no follow-up question. Apparently satisfied, she turns and continues climbing the steps, and Morgan follows.
The passage makes several more turns, carving out an erratic, squared squiggle. Morgan takes no further notes. Her knees are about ready to explode by the time they reach the top.

They emerge, blinking at the light, on a wet, windswept, slightly slanted plateau. There are more of the tiny tombstone indentations underfoot. The edges of the monolith are some distance away but they are not marked; the dark grey surface just ends at a straight line not far out, and the horizon itself is below it, not visible. This gives Morgan some vertigo, particularly since the surface tilts toward one corner, and the engraved basalt underfoot is slick, wet and getting wetter.

There is a small cluster of Organisation scientific equipment up here, chunky weatherproof units stacked up under a canopy. There is a table, with a rugged, beaten-up computer terminal, switched off. Farther away is a diesel generator.

Quinn ignores the equipment and paces off in a different direction, facing away from Morgan and out at the sky, playing with her cigarette lighter, although not actually lighting anything. The lighter is actually a tiny propane burner intended for lighting stoves, given to her by her mother before she died. Quinn no longer remembers this.

Morgan waits for a while, arms folded for warmth, gradually getting wetter. She doesn’t seek cover under the canopy, because Quinn hasn’t. She senses that something is about to happen. Quinn is normally quite poised and difficult to read, but she looks apprehensive; upset, even. Focused intently on the lighter flame, Quinn seems to be unable to look her in the eye, as if she doesn’t want to push through with the next part of whatever this is actually supposed to be. Orientation? Initiation? Hazing?
What was that about getting to the point?
‘It’s a memorial,’ Morgan says.
‘Hhn.’ Quinn snaps the lighter shut and pockets it, moderately impressed. Only moderately, though. ‘That’s right. Of course, I practically told you that, when I mentioned tombstones –’
‘How many Antimemetics Wars have there been?’
That gets her. ‘So much for slow-burning theatrics. Someone told you? You read the entry?’
Morgan looks at her shoes. ‘Uhm. No. Really, I’ve never seen this place before. I was just guessing.’
‘You look embarrassed,’ Quinn says. ‘You’re embarrassed that you hit the right answer thirty minutes before I was expecting you to. You think you’ve shown me up. Right? Oli. Look at me.’
She looks.

‘Keep operating at that level. Don’t slow down for my benefit, or anyone’s. I hire people who don’t think the way I think. My background’s in psychology. That’s why I hire botanists. And set theorists. And Egyptologists.’ She gestures at Morgan. ‘People with alternate perspectives. People who can take problems the rest of us have been hammering on for ten years and see straight through them.’
‘Will you tell me why we’re here?’ Morgan asks, for what she hopes will be the final time.

‘The problem,’ Quinn says, ‘is that every single person in the world with reliable access to high-grade mnestic medication works for me, here. And the Division is pitifully understaffed. There are one hundred and forty of us, including you and me, and one hundred and forty pairs of eyes is not enough. We cannot look at enough of the world at once. There is an appallingly large percentage of the world that no human has ever properly looked at. This is unbearably limiting to all forms of antimemetic research. Antimemetic history, antimemetic paleontology, antimemetic cosmology … These disciplines, all of them, barely exist. They are nowhere.

‘Nevertheless, we have seen this culture’s cities. One or two still exist. Pure dumb luck is how we found them. A Division researcher visits the U-9010 complex in Nevada, drives back across the Mojave while still on the dose … sees something on the horizon. That sort of thing. The cities are physically ruined, and there are heavy antimemetic effects shrouding them that make them nearly impossible to study, even for us. Large, simple things, like this stone, survived better, but even so … We think this stone was one of the last things they built before they died out.

‘They were human beings. They were probably significantly more technologically advanced than we are. They existed thousands of years ago; perhaps tens of thousands, we can’t know for sure. It’s difficult to determine what really happened to them because their entire cultural memeplex was lethally irradiated. Their core cultural concepts, the things they created, and stood for, and valued highly, can never be known or propagated again.

‘We think an idea stole into their culture that they did not have adaptations to defend against. A Category Epsilon idea complex. They may have fought it. But, ultimately, their culture is buried beneath that idea.’
Quinn pauses, letting the rain patter for a significant moment.
‘Millions of people died,’ Morgan says.
Quinn says nothing.

‘And we forgot about this?’ Morgan asks. ‘The rest of us. The fraction of the fraction, who survived the War, and became modern humanity. You and me and everybody. We, what, looked away? And walked away, and “moved on”?’
‘Yes.’
Morgan staggers, vertigo swelling up and briefly getting the better of her. ‘Millions of people died. And we just forgot. Is that what you wanted to show me? You want me to write that down?’

‘Yes,’ Quinn says. ‘Yes. Write this down. You’re learning two things today, and this is the first. Humans can forget anything. It’s okay to forget some things, because we are mortal and finite. But some things we have to remember. It’s important that we remember. Write something that will make you remember.’

Morgan nods. It’s raining too heavily, so she retreats under the canopy and uses the table. Even so, a few raindrops spatter her notes. She writes intently and rapidly, for some time. What she writes is rushed and unrefined, with large parts crossed out. She wonders how she’ll react when she reads it for the first time.
After a while, Quinn joins her under the canopy.
Morgan, staring at her notes, asks Quinn, as if she doesn’t already know the answer: ‘And the second thing?’
Quinn says:
‘It is possible that their culture had an equivalent to the Organisation. It may even have had an Antimemetics Division. If they did, their Organisation, and their Antimemetics Division, failed them.

‘It’s a big reality. It’s a huge Organisation, much bigger than you’re cleared to know. There’s a lot of Cat. Epsilons, and a lot of war-gamed scenarios where those Cat. Epsilons break containment and the orbital contingencies fail and an eye-watering number of people end up dead. And most of those Epsilons are handled by other divisions, on other continents. So, maybe the end of the world will be some other division’s problem. And yes, a big part of the job we hired you for is basic research. Lab work, as safe as it gets. And yes, it’s been thousands of years, and it may be thousands of years more.
‘But maybe it won’t. And maybe it will be our problem.

‘Organisation staff join Antimemetics for a lot of reasons, and one of those reasons is that they perceive the work to be easier. Safer. Lower-stakes. But an idea can end a world. An idea is alive. It can mutate, it can replicate, it can predate on other ideas … and it can hide. And – people deserve to be safe. This is the purpose of the Organisation. It’s what we are for. Research, yes, Unknown science, because that helps, but ultimately we are here to keep everybody safe. Not just from scalpel monsters and optically communicable fasciitis and the Cult of the Holocene Impactor. From everything!’
Quinn is amped up. She realises it at the same instant that Morgan notices it. Her usual cold facade has slipped, and she has betrayed some earnest motivation. She takes a second, cools off.
‘To answer your original question,’ she says, ‘there has been one Antimemetics War that we know of. Potentially others that we don’t know of. And there is, undoubtedly, one to come.’

Morgan says nothing. She looks dismayed, broken. She’s right to be, and Quinn is familiar with the reaction. This is, indeed, part of every new Antimemetics Division operative’s orientation. The magnitude of responsibility can be hard to handle. It’s meant to be.
‘Welcome to the Antimemetics Division,’ Quinn says. ‘This is your first day.’
Morgan writes for some time longer. Quinn waits, silently. The rain doesn’t let up.
‘But what was it?’ Morgan asks. ‘What was the idea?’

‘U-9429-B,’ Quinn says. ‘We isolated the memeplex itself in the seventies. We have the descriptor on ice in a Vegas room, basement level two. It’s harmless now, Cat. 1. It’s so culturally alien to modern humans as to be nearly incoherent. Think like Egyptian hieroglyphs. I’ll show you another day.’
‘Are you saying it couldn’t come back?’
‘In that form, it’s highly unlikely. Our culture just isn’t shaped like that anymore.’
Morgan stares at her notes. She’s drawn a sigil at the bottom, something Quinn can’t immediately identify.
‘I study ancient cultures,’ Morgan says, distantly. ‘I can read hieroglyphs. Some of them.’
The sigil isn’t a hieroglyph, as far as Quinn can tell. But then, Quinn isn’t a specialist. She hires specialists. That’s the whole point …
‘What does it look like?’ Morgan asks.
‘Yes.’

An idea doesn’t look like anything, Quinn goes to say, but stops herself. Morgan doesn’t know it, but in the database entry for U-9429-B there are complex plots, visualisations of what it looks like as a constellation in memetic space. Now that she thinks about it, U-9429-B has numerous aspects. Symmetries, even.
‘Ugly,’ she says. ‘Fascinating,’ she adds. ‘Why?’
Morgan is staring into the distance, now.
Quinn follows her gaze. There’s nothing out there. Just overcast sky and rain.
And after a few seconds, Quinn’s gut instinct kicks her, as it has many times before, hard enough to make her jump. Something is wrong here. A piece is missing from the scene.
She turns back to Morgan. ‘Oli, why?’
Morgan points at something, far away in the sky. Her breathing is becoming heavier.
Quinn sees nothing. ‘What do you see? Under heavy mnestic doses, some people say they see ghosts here. We even have some supposed interview logs. Personally, I think their veracity is dubious …’

‘Um. It doesn’t look like a ghost. It looks like a … a half-starved … kaiju. A monster. A pillar made of spiders. It’s taller than this stone. At least twice as tall. It’s coming here. Is this normal?’
‘No.’ Quinn is already racing through the checklist.
‘What is it?’
‘I don’t know.’
‘This isn’t part of the hazing?’

‘No. I will never lie to you, Oli. I swear.’ An antimemetically cloaked entity as monstrous as Morgan’s description has a zero percent chance of being benign. They need support. Quinn finds that her phone has no signal. Morgan’s will be the same. The only way to get a message out of here is with a written note. A paper airplane, thrown off the top into the woods?

‘It’s bending down. I think it’s looking at me,’ Morgan says, watching a space in the air descend. There isn’t even a hole in the rain that Quinn can perceive. ‘Its head is gigantic, it has to be ten metres wide. It has … graspers and arthropod legs all over it. Dozens of eyes. Some of them are blinded. There’s someone riding it.’
‘What? Describe the rider.’

‘Caucasian male, twenties, skinny. Jeans, trainers, dirty brown hair, needs a haircut. He’s been shot. He’s bleeding out all over but he doesn’t seem to notice. In the liver, and again in the throat, just above the clavicle. He’s smiling.’

Quinn spends a second wondering whether the gunshot wounds are intentionally creepy detailing, or whether the man is genuinely using some kind of advanced antimemetic power to ignore a mortal wound. And, if the latter, how, and how he originally sustained it. But more urgent questions are afoot. ‘He sees you?’
‘Yes.’
‘Does he see me? Hear me?’
Morgan is transfixed and is starting to look genuinely frightened. ‘He wants to know who I’m talking to.’

‘Don’t tell him. He doesn’t get information about us, understand?’ Quinn pulls her walkie-talkie from her waist, sets it to broadcast an emergency beacon, turns and hurls it overarm as far as she possibly can, in the direction of the Wyeleigh main building. With luck, it’ll land intact in the forest, outside the suppression zone cast by U-9429, summoning a Hazardous Response Team. ‘Ask who he is.’
Morgan is standing very still, with her arms clamped rigidly at her side. ‘Who are you? … He says … he says he’s nearly finished. He says he’s going to kill me.’
‘Like hell. Oli, listen to me. We’re running for it. Back down the steps. If we can get to the perimetre of the stone it’ll flush our memories.’
‘I can’t move.’
Quinn hauls on one of Morgan’s arms. She can’t be moved. ‘Put one foot in front of the other!’
‘It’s got ahold of me.’ Morgan is goggle-eyed and starting to hyperventilate.

Quinn disengages and surveys the situation. She can’t see or touch any grasping spider legs, or the monstrous face that Morgan can’t look away from, or the rider. But she believes Morgan that they’re there, real for some value of ‘real.’ She claps one hand to her side; but of course she isn’t carrying her sidearm, because U-9429 is a Cat. 1 Unknown, and this is home territory, and why would she be? Not that it even makes a difference when this mythical rider is able to laugh off gunshot wounds. There aren’t enough options in front of her. She very badly wants to swear, and bites down hard on her tongue.
Morgan screams.
‘Oli!’ Quinn shouts. ‘Don’t look at it. Look at me.’
‘I can’t.’
‘You’re stronger than this.’
‘I’m not,’ Morgan cries.
‘You’re the best we have,’ Quinn says. ‘I’m not making that up. You saw this thing when nobody else could. That makes you smarter and stronger. You can fight it. Concentrate!’
‘He hates us so much,’ Morgan says. ‘I can’t think through it. I can’t see. Please. Please don’t.’

Quinn circles behind Morgan, plants one hand on her shoulder for stability, and punches her behind the ear. Morgan sags in place, then falls forward to her knees. Quinn is just about able to catch her before her skull connects with the ground.

But she didn’t hit her hard enough. Morgan is unconscious only for a second. She struggles as she comes back. It’s like she’s waking from a nightmare into another nightmare. She clutches at Quinn’s hand. She can’t scream. Her heart stops.
Quinn rolls her over and administers CPR, but without equipment there’s very little chance of her restarting Morgan’s heart.
Nobody’s coming. She didn’t throw the walkie-talkie far enough.
It’s almost ten minutes before she gives up.

And then Quinn is collapsed against the wall of the passage, on the next-to-last step, about to leave U-9429’s field of influence, trying to figure out what in the fuck she can possibly write to herself.
It’s back. It’s here. It never died. We just couldn’t see it. Has it mutated?

Morgan could see it. All she did was look at it and it killed her. She was as good as any of us. She was as capable as she was ever going to be and she wasn’t good enough. How do you fight an antimemetic monster that only eats the best antimemeticists?

You … you could try to build some kind of countermeme. But you’d need to be shielded while you worked on it. You’d need a hermetically sealed, self-sustaining lab as big as an arcology. Like the ones Ed Hix used to build. Like … the one under the Wyeleigh labs. Basement level 30.
God. How long have we been fighting this thing?

There’s a rustling behind her. She turns to look. Far away up the steps, there he is, the rider Morgan described. A scrawny young man with a hostile frown and, yes, two steadily oozing gunshot wounds. His shoes are soaked in blood.
He calls out, ‘Marie Quinn! I owe you for the lake.’
Quinn stands up. She doesn’t know what lake he’s talking about. But she says nothing.

The rider gestures. Blue and brown and black spiders of all sizes cascade around the corner, flooding the passageway up to his knees, pouring over his shoulders, tumbling down toward Quinn. They make a strange, organic rustling as they pour, like wet leaves. There must be millions of them. The spiders would probably be much more effective if she was at all afraid of them.

It’s too bad. She’s just learned a great deal about this entity; that they have history together, and that it personally dislikes her, and that it apparently has a humanoid mouthpiece … and a poor imagination. But she has only a second before the cascade of arachnids overcomes her, and that’s not enough time to even write a single word. Morgan’s death, then, was for nothing.
She steps backward, over the threshold.
The rain is finally easing off. Quinn lights a cigarette and heads back to the main building. It’s almost time for her weekly inspection of U-2200.

chapters.title: 2: Where Have You Been All My Life
paragraphs: Where Have You Been All My Life
Who the fuck infiltrates a senior Organisation official’s home, while they’re home?

Marie Quinn lives deep in the forest, a long drive from the nearest major city and a long drive in the opposite direction from Wyeleigh. It’s late, last thing, and she’s reading in bed when she hears the muffled, unmistakable click of her front door being unlocked. She looks up, and stares blankly at the wall for a second while listening to soft footsteps moving into the hallway.

She marks her place and reaches for her Organisation-issued phone. She has no permanent security staff at home – the Division is understaffed and trained operatives are in much more serious need at Wyeleigh proper – but the building and grounds have beefy electronic countermeasures. They, she discovers, have all been disabled, along with the sensors and cameras. She was not notified that this had happened. Whoever did it had a valid code.
Who, though?

The Organisation has enemies. True, the list of credible, motivated enemies is short, and the list of groups stupid enough to try to kill or capture someone at her level is shorter. But it’s far from empty, and it’s not actually so hard a feat; not too many Organisation staff below the C-level have the privilege of travelling by motorcade. The real trick, the impossible trick, is to avoid unholy retaliation. But what if you really think you can? What if you’ve decided it’s worth it?
Quinn triggers the silent alarm. Ten minutes to the response team. Maybe eight, but realistically ten. Either figure is an eternity. She’s on her own.

She rolls out of bed, tucks a few pillows in her place, and casts around, hopelessly, for a weapon. Her sidearm is in the safe under the stairs and there are knives, good ones, in the kitchen. She can’t get to them. She is surrounded by soft furnishings. She clenches her fists, mouthing profanity.
She opens a seldom-used lower drawer. She throws a few bundles of yarn aside, and pulls out a steel knitting needle.

Then she moves silently to her bedroom door, gripping the needle in her right fist, thumb on the knob. The ‘sharp’ end of the needle is pitifully blunt but with a suitable amount of aggression she bets she can drive it through, for example, someone’s trachea.

Now she listens, and thinks. This door, her bedroom door, can’t be opened silently. It creaks like hell, so if she goes through it she’ll have to be ready to draw attention. There’s an attic, but access is out there on the landing and, again, can’t be operated silently. There’s no alternate route to ground level other than jumping from the window, and she assumes that someone is covering it. Even if she landed in the bushes alive, she’d still have to break the perimetre with a sprained ankle.
A creaking. This damned house. Someone is coming up the stairs, making no effort to be quiet about it. A soft tread, though. As if they removed their shoes. Just one of them? That barely makes sense.

It’s too late for the rest of the logic. The door’s opening. It seems like the man’s trying to say something as he comes in, but he only gets as far as ‘I – whulp,’ and it’s done. He’s flat on his face, cheek pressed into deep cream carpet, with Quinn on his back pinning both his wrists with her knees. She glances back down the stairs for a second; there’s no one there. She prods him in his other cheek with the tip of the knitting needle. ‘You speak or move, I’ll put a hole in your throat,’ she hisses. She glances at the windows, checks the stairs again, listens intently. There’s no sound. There’s nothing to be seen.

The man is fifty, and lanky. He wears an expensive dark suit, tailored to his build. He has angular features, thick, greying hair and rimless spectacles, now quite possibly bent out of shape by their sudden impact with the floor. He wears discreet platinum jewelry: a wristwatch, cufflinks and a ring.
The two of them halt like that, a tableau. He makes no attempt to move, although he does look askance at Quinn, as best he can given his dislodged glasses.
Quinn asks, ‘Where are the others?’
‘It’s just me, Marie,’ he answers.
‘Who are you?’
He says nothing for a moment, but his expression slowly, subtly drops. ‘I, ah. Well. Well, it really happened, didn’t it? I always wondered.’
‘Who are you?’

‘There is a monster that follows you around and eats your memories,’ the man says. ‘U-4987. You drip-feed it inconsequential trivia so it doesn’t go after anything important. You watch quiz shows. The book you were reading just now. On your nightstand. It’s a trivia book. Right?’
Quinn says nothing to confirm or deny this, although it is true. At feeding time the entity manifests like a bright gold-white spot in the corner of her eye. It’s gone now.
She’s already put the rest of it together. It is all mind-bogglingly, insultingly obvious.
The man goes on, ‘We call it “Sunshine.”’
Fighting to keep the note of dismay out of her voice, she asks, ‘What’s your name?’
‘Adam,’ he says. ‘Adam Quinn.’
Obviously, she has the man detained.

She instructs her people to interrogate him – lightly – and to run deep background research on every word he utters, while for her part she stands far back from the investigation to avoid contamination. She resists the urge to interfere, particularly to visit ‘Adam’ and personally demand answers. She goes to her office, curls up on the couch there and tries to catch some sleep, but doesn’t succeed in any real sense.

Seven hours later Geoff Ives knocks on her office door, bringing a three-centimetre-thick block of printouts and a paralysingly strong cup of coffee. Quinn takes the drink first, accepting it as a kind of authentication step before letting the man in. She moves back to the couch and sits hunched over the drink for warmth, inhaling its fumes.

Ives settles heavily into a chair opposite the couch. ‘They, uh,’ he says, ‘reckoned I should be the one to present their results. Even though I didn’t research one line of it. “Cause you and I “get along.” Their words. Personally, I don’t see it.’
Quinn stays focused on the coffee. ‘Who is he?’

Ives opens the first page of the report and takes a breath. ‘“Adam Benjamin Quinn. Born the twenty-seventh of February 1962 in Holme, Derbyshire, to Margaret Edith Quinn, née Wachs’ – doubt I’m pronouncing that correctly – ‘and Patrick John Quinn, both now deceased. No siblings. Attended Holme Church of England Primary School, Clapbrook All Saints Secondary. Demonstrated great musical acuity from an early age. By age sixteen he had begun to be recognised as one of the most gifted classical violinists of his generation. Attended the Royal College of –”’
‘Skip,’ Quinn says. ‘Skip, skip.’

Ives turns a few pages, looking for something more directly relevant to Quinn’s interests. He quotes: ‘“After sustaining a minor injury whilst on a concert tour in the north-west of England, he encountered what the Organisation now designates as Unknown 8051, which had infested a wing of the hospital where he received treatment.” That’s the Digit Thief. “His attempt to alert authorities to the infestation’s presence was intercepted by an Organisation listening station. Operative Marie A. Sheridan, then a field operative based in –”’
‘Skip. Summarise.’
Ives skips to the end. ‘Quote: “They subsequently became romantically involved.” Unquote.’ He shrugs lightly, and closes the report and puts it aside.
There is a heavy pause.
‘I remember acquiring U-8051,’ Quinn says. ‘There was no one else there.’ It’s difficult to say who, if anybody, she’s trying to convince.

‘He is your husband,’ Ives says. ‘Every word checks out. There is limitless physical evidence. In the report I’ve seen school certificates, dental records, gas bills, holiday photos.’ He chooses not to add wedding photos. He could swear there was a wedding photograph on Quinn’s desk at one point, but he doesn’t see it.

‘And that’s just the physical evidence,’ he goes on. ‘We know him. Half the Division senior staff has met him socially, including me. Barbecues and the like. Partners are invited along. Right?’ He leans forward and adds, in a low voice, ‘More to the point, it doesn’t make sense to fabricate such an extensive physical backstory and backfill everybody’s memories except for yours. And be discovered instantly? Even if there was something in the world that could do a thing like that, which isn’t completely impossible, there’s no threat model that leads to it.

‘He’s real. Do you believe that he’s real, Marie? I want us to be on the same page on this one. We’re not filing this man away forever under a four-digit number. You adhered to protocol today, and I respect that, but the bottom line is that U-4987 got hungry.’
Quinn nods. The assessment matches her own, pieced together overnight from gut reactions and analysis of the plain facts.
Where else did her name come from? She wasn’t born ‘Quinn.’ But she had to get independent verification.

Ives says, ‘One other thing. He was carrying this. It was in his glasses case.’ From his pocket, he takes out an autoinjector pen: a chunky white cylinder with a luminous orange vial mounted inside it and a cap concealing a needle. He passes it to Quinn.
She inspects it. The pen is generic, with no visible branding, but she recognises the design. It is UO-made. The vial is unmarked except for a bold black Z.
Ives says, ‘He knows exactly what it is. And how to use it. He says you gave it to him. For emergencies.’
‘… Well, that confirms it,’ Quinn murmurs. ‘I trusted him. My God.’
‘Director,’ Ives says. There is a note of carefully restrained anger.
Quinn looks over her glasses at him. ‘Say what you need to say.’

‘A civilian can’t have this,’ Ives says. ‘This substance is extremely strictly controlled and extremely dangerous. There have been multiple proposals to destroy the entire stockpile. For excellent reasons.’
She grimaces, but acknowledges the point. ‘After we’re through here, I’ll return it to containment.’
This placates him. Somewhat. ‘With respect, ma’am: What were you thinking?’
‘I don’t know what I was thinking,’ Quinn snaps. ‘I’m not her anymore! I don’t know who he is! Has this’ – she gestures at her head – ‘happened before?’
‘To our knowledge, no.’
‘No, of course not. Could it happen again?’
Ives shrugs. ‘You would know better than anyone.’

‘I have U-4987 trained to follow me at my heel. I feed it according to a strict regimen. It eats only the memories I say it’s okay to eat. A rapidly progressive, universally fatal memory parasite made chronic and then domesticated. And now, what, it suddenly breaks training? That adds up?’

‘If you say it doesn’t add up, it doesn’t add up,’ Ives says, cautiously. ‘You’ve lived with it, in peace, for a long time. But speaking as a field operative, trusting an Unknown to behave predictably is a phenomenal way to get written off.’

Quinn has waited long enough, and takes a long pull from the coffee. She stares into the coiling steam, as if trying to see the future. ‘But who is he?’ she asks again. ‘At this point, you know him better than I do. What’s he like? Do you like him?’
Ives grimaces extravagantly. This is the great-grandmother of all loaded questions.
Quinn looks him in the eye and says, ‘Tell me your personal impression of Adam Quinn. Direct order.’
‘… He’s a nice enough guy.’
‘“Nice enough”?’

Ives clicks his tongue. ‘I don’t like him,’ he admits. ‘Personally. All that much. We’re civil. But he will always be a little bit too smug, and a little bit too clever. He just … grates. Would I leave someone in a cell for that? No.’
‘Do I like him?’

‘You –’ Ives begins, then stops. He looks away. And over time, a soft smile develops on his face, one that Quinn doesn’t recall ever seeing before, not in a working relationship going back years. ‘Yeah,’ he says. ‘Yeah. He’s the one.’

Quinn is reading the report alone, now. The report details the U-8051 acquisition, and Adam Quinn’s role in it, in the most sterile, disinterested terms conceivable. She learns nothing at all from it about his personality, and reading it feels like dying.
She remembers acquiring U-8051. She remembers that there was no one there.
She remembers incorrectly.

There’s another conglomeration of severed fingers in the last room, coating the room’s interior like the innards of an exploded elephant. Parts of the sprawl are feeling their way, like mold, into a medical cabinet and the rest is splayed over a fetal shape on a medical gurney. The mass reacts sharply to the new light as Adam Quinn opens the door, rearing up and angling parts of itself toward him. He reels backward and pulls the door closed just in time; there is a heavy, fleshy thump as the mass hits the door from the far side. The door holds.

Quinn trips on his own foot and slumps against the far wall. The shape on the gurney was a coiled-up human. Not a corpse, but a living human with one wide-open eye whose whole body was being slowly consumed and processed into more fingers. They were growing out of his throat. Quinn didn’t see this. He thinks he saw it, but he knows he couldn’t have.
And that’s it. Quinn casts around the corridor. Every other door he’s tried is blocked or locked. The place is belowground, so no windows. No navigable ventilation.

There are two more gunshots up at the far end of the corridor, ear-splitting in the enclosed space and echoing for many seconds. Sheridan rounds the corner at a dead run, gun in hand, and reaches him quickly. ‘Find a way out?’ she asks, pointlessly. She can read Quinn’s expression. He’s found nothing good.
‘This place is infested,’ Quinn says. ‘Every room, all the stairwells … This is absurd.’

At the far end of the corridor, the main mass heaves itself around the corner. From this distance, it looks like an ambulatory eight-ton pile of moldy mashed potato and fat, wiggling maggots. There are toes in there as well as fingers, and small teeth, and bits of bone. It has twenty bullet holes in it, and blood is flowing from all of them, but if it has vital organs they must be elsewhere in the building because none of the wounds have slowed it down or otherwise altered its slow, methodical homing behaviour. It smells powerfully and creatively disgusting, like concentrated medical waste.

It lurches forward in intermittent phases, coating the walls and floor with scarlet ooze as it moves. It’ll be on them in about half a minute, squashing them against the end of the corridor and then pulling them into the mess to be remade.
‘I think we’re done,’ Quinn quavers. ‘Thanks for trying.’
Sheridan, for her part, just stands there, gun lowered, watching the thing come. It moves slowly, like a steamroller. It fills the corridor almost to the ceiling.

She has two bullets left and she’s considering where to spend them. Shooting the mass itself is like shooting pudding. She’d kill for a grenade. Even a fire axe would be something. She might not be able to stop the thing, but she could at least make herself known with a fire axe. She could make it feel some regret.
‘There are worse fates, I guess,’ Quinn goes on, finding himself unable to stop talking, ‘than being digitised by that thing, but not all that many.’
Sheridan glances in his direction, apparently paying him direct attention for the first time since they met, sixty crowded minutes ago. She says, ‘Riser cupboard.’
‘What?’

She pushes Quinn aside. There’s a white-painted wall behind him. There is a long vertical seam separating the wall into two floor-to-ceiling panels, and a lock just beside the seam. Sheridan spends a moment choosing the right part of the lock to shoot, and shoots it out. Both panels open to reveal a shallow, dusty, metal-edged space, like an elevator shaft with no elevator, allowing filthy pipes and cables to pass vertically between floors. She looks up. There’s just enough room to admit a person.

‘Can you climb?’ she asks Quinn. Without waiting for a response, she sheds her suit jacket, sticks a torch between her teeth, and hauls herself up into the darkness. After a brief moment of scuffling, there’s another gunshot: the riser cupboard door on the next floor up.
‘No,’ Quinn finally manages. ‘No, I can’t climb!’ The mass is almost on him. He’s transfixed by its motion, its all-too-familiar grasping behaviour.
‘I figured,’ Sheridan calls down. A hand descends, a human one with the conventional number of fingers. ‘It’s clear up here. Come on, I’m braced. Mind this lip here, it’s metal. Come on!’

Quinn keeps his own jacket on and buttoned; it’s the only part of the situation over which he still has firm control. He has to jump to catch hold of Sheridan’s hand, and just as he jumps, the main mass lunges for him, crossing the last few metres in a rush and catching hold of him by one foot.
He sees himself die.

His sweating hand immediately starts to slip out of Sheridan’s. She braces her other arm and hauls him up fifteen or thirty centimetres with an angry grunt, then releases his hand for a split second and reaches down like a flash to take firmer hold of his wrist. She keeps pulling. The mass closes around Quinn’s foot like aggressive, proactive quicksand. He yelps and kicks at it with his other foot until it finally pries his shoe loose. The mass retreats for a second, taking a moment to realise that its prize is not living flesh, but by that time Sheridan has hauled Quinn up another half metre and Quinn has started pushing himself upward off the pipework with his feet. The mass lunges again, but falls short, and seems too unintelligent to climb after them. It sloshes around, probing its surroundings, perplexed by the shoe.

Sheridan hauls Quinn over the lip into the next corridor. He scrapes his ribs badly and arrives crawling, eyes watering. He doesn’t die. He can still see himself dying. He stays on all fours for a significant amount of time, processing what just happened.
‘Fuck!’
Sheridan is already standing, and apparently not even significantly exerted. ‘We need to get to the roof. I might be able to get a signal out from there.’
‘You’re at the gym pretty often?’ Quinn pants, sitting back. ‘You train for nonsense like this?’
‘Yeah.’
‘That’s wonderful,’ Quinn says, ‘because I play the violin. It’s not quite as physically demanding, as careers go. When you said you were a health inspector, that was an enormous lie, wasn’t it?’
Sheridan ignores the question, out of habit, and waits impassively for the man to cool.

‘This is asinine,’ Quinn declares. ‘This is brain damage.’ His skin crawls, and grotesque visions flood through his brain. Eventually he recovers his breath and gets to his feet. He stands lopsided, so he takes his other shoe off and throws it back down the riser for symmetry.
‘We need to get to the roof,’ Sheridan says again.

Quinn blinks a long blink, then focuses on something around the corner, something on the wall that Sheridan can’t see from where she’s standing. ‘Yes. One second.’ He goes to it – it’s a red panel – and pulls something down. ‘Here, you were having no luck with the gun. Try this.’
It’s a fire axe.

He stepped on a rusty nail backstage after the show, and came to the hospital for a tetanus injection. While waiting, he slowly realised that more than half of the people waiting with him were clutching partially or entirely severed fingers. Bandsaw accidents; hands caught in car doors; hands trapped in door hinges; hands crushed in machinery; every one of them unrelated. There was an epidemic of physical injury, which should have been impossible, and when he tried to bring it up with the medical staff they didn’t seem to understand what he was saying. It was like a nightmare. A very personal nightmare, to Quinn, nightmarish in its similarity to real nightmares he had from time to time, about losing the use of his own fingers.

Then he saw one of them escape. He followed it as it wriggled away down a long corridor to a far corner of the hospital, to an ajar door that nobody in the hospital seemed to be able to perceive except for him, and into a different building where there were no people at all, just hundreds and hundreds of wriggling, exploring, slowly reproducing and lengthening fingers.

He slammed the door and tried and failed to get someone, anyone, staff or patient, to see what he was seeing. He found a pay-phone and dialed emergency services and ordered off the menu, asking for emergency industrial-scale pest control or hazardous containment or psychic support or something.

And there was a long pause, and he was connected to what was either a very measured, dispassionate human or an impressively articulate robot operator. It told him to wait by the phone; an associate would be with him shortly. Marie Sheridan arrived in person, slightly less than fifteen minutes later.

He showed her the door. They went a few paces inside, Sheridan crouching and aiming some kind of scanner at the finger worms. Behind them, something reached out and gently pushed the door closed with a click. They turned, and saw what it was, and ran.

Sheridan hacks her way through the last of the flesh-clogged stairwell. They’re almost at the roof. This part of the distributed infestation doesn’t seem to be mobile, although it is freakishly grabby.

Quinn stands three paces back from her, partly to avoid the backswing but mostly so he doesn’t have to watch. It’s butchery, and it’s grisly, and Sheridan barely seems perturbed by it; she slices methodically until there are waterfalls of gore coming down the stairs and soaking her shoes and his socks, and she does it with the manner of someone trimming a hedgerow.
Whunch. Krunlch.

Quinn is shivering, and starting to crash. If he doesn’t stay still right in the middle of the stairwell, the remaining fingers tug at his hair and sleeves. In another few minutes it may finally dawn on him that this is really happening. ‘This is bonkers, this is nuts,’ he says to himself, over and over.
‘What was that word you used back there?’ Sheridan asks, suddenly.
‘Mmm?’
Whunch. ‘Don’t tune out. When the mass was coming down the hall. Did you say “digitised”?’
‘… Um.’ Quinn seems to change gear, and wake up. ‘Yeah. Uh, but, in the old sense of the word –’
‘“Digit” meaning “finger,” so “digitised” meaning “turned into fingers.” I just got it.’ She’s smiling, he can hear it. Chlunk. ‘That’s great.’
‘I … Thank you?’
‘What kind of violin music?’

‘Uh. What kind would you like? Tonight’s – last night’s – Christ, yesterday’s concert was Prokofiev’s Violin Concerto No. 1. And a few other pieces, obviously, but that was the main course for me. That was where I got my teeth in.’
Sheridan stops hacking and turns around. She actually looks him in the eye. ‘That piece is a nightmare.’
‘It’s a challenge,’ Quinn admits, brightly.
‘No, I mean it’s chaotic. It’s unlistenable.’
‘I can play anything you like,’ Quinn states.
Sheridan appears to spend a moment considering this possibility. ‘Bach. You can play some Bach?’
‘Of course. Just get me to a violin.’
Sheridan thinks for a moment longer. She nods, and goes back to hacking.

And they hit the roof, and Sheridan’s radio finally works, and she calls everything in. She speaks in rapid keywords that Quinn can’t quite follow, although he can pick out his own name and ‘hazmat’ and a repeated word that sounds to him like a brand of cassette tape: ‘Memetix.’

It’s very nearly dawn. This wing of the hospital is a few storeys shorter than its main body, so rows of bright-lit wards look down over the roof, while the roof looks out over two sprawling car parks and then greenery and roads and a faint, dull red where the Sun is due to come up. Sheridan quickly ascertains that there is no fire escape from here; the intended fire exit from the roof is the stairwell they just used to get up here, so they’ll have to wait for a helicopter. Or, more likely and less romantically, a long ladder.

‘Backup is coming,’ Sheridan concludes. ‘They have to come in from the next city over, so it could be a while. They’ll have decontamination gear, antibiotics, blankets, tedious debriefing forms, you name it. But, most importantly, coffee.’

Quinn makes an inarticulate sound, the sound of one who could use the coffee, and after that, a drink. ‘God, I have another concert today,’ he says. He sits on the thick perimetre wall, rubs his eyes, rubs his sore feet, and begins to shut down.
‘You’ll be there,’ Sheridan says. ‘The nasty part is over. You did well for a civilian. I’ve seen far worse.’
‘Worse than this?’
Sheridan says nothing.
‘I’m sorry.’ Quinn opens his eyes again. He gestures at the mayhem from which they just escaped, the fire door and everything it leads to. It’s all still down there. ‘You’ve seen worse than this?’
Sheridan, again, says nothing.
‘What is this? What happened here?’
At first Sheridan doesn’t answer this either. She walks away across the roof and spends an entire minute staring at the forthcoming Sun.
And then, surprising Quinn and slightly surprising even herself, she walks back to him and says:

‘U-8051, which is the reference number we just assigned to this infestation, has an intrinsic property that makes it nearly impossible for sapient organisms to perceive it. It’s a form of camouflage. It’s not invisible, it’s a mental blocking effect. Information about it goes nowhere, it gets suppressed. People walk past this building every day of the week. They don’t see what’s blocking the windows. They walk past that door you saw, and don’t realise it’s standing open. It could have been here for decades. The researchers will get the whole story eventually.’
Quinn finds in this explanation something he halfway understands. ‘So … living fnords?’

And this actually slows Sheridan down for a second. She gets that reference. She read those books when she was younger, years ago, before joining the Organisation. But she’s never made the connection between fnords and the work she does. For as long as she’s been working there, she hasn’t even thought about it. The irony is intense enough to burn.
‘Yeah,’ she says.
‘Except that you can see them,’ Quinn says.
‘I have specialist training,’ Sheridan says, declining to mention her drug regimen.
‘And I, also, can see them.’

‘You seem to have a mild natural immunity to memory-clouding phenomena,’ Sheridan explains. ‘It’s rare, but it happens. At a hospital this busy, someone like you was bound to stumble into this place sooner or later.’ And escape alive, she adds, privately. ‘But the point is … this infestation, Unknown 8051, is a snowflake. I don’t just mean that it’s special and unique. I mean: it’s part of a blizzard.

‘We’ve been collecting Unknowns for about as long as we’ve known what science is. When empiricism and the scientific method were first starting to take shape, we realised that there were phenomena in the world that actively defied reason. Violations, weirdnesses, defects. Inexplicable curios, creatures, people, places, events, ideas. So we formed a society and we started gathering them and studying them. The world was like some grand unbalanced equation, with known science on one side and all these Unknowns on the other.

‘As time passed, the amount of known science grew and the number of Unknowns grew in lockstep. They grew more numerous and more creative and more frightening. And then something happened. Something I’m not cleared for, but that struck the fear of God into the people running the Society at the time. And it stopped being a secretive academic pursuit. We had to get organised.

‘We are researchers. Mostly. Still. Researchers with a global mandate, formidable resources, and … unimaginable responsibilities. We watch the blizzard. And we guard the little fire. We’ve been doing it for about two hundred years.’

Quinn’s full attention is on her now. He feels tense and exposed here, vulnerable to extraordinary natural forces from which by rights he should be fleeing. But he’s also fascinated. Sheridan has a faintly ethereal attitude to her. It’s as if she’s not standing on the same planet as everybody else.

She says, ‘It’s about fear. Fundamentally. People shouldn’t have to be afraid. Of hunger, sickness, violence, injustice. But especially, people shouldn’t have to be afraid of things like that.’ She points. ‘So that’s what we do. We take care of it. So you can sleep at night.’
That’s it. That’s the moment, although Quinn won’t realise it until years later.
‘… So you’re not with the FBI,’ he says. ‘This wouldn’t be the right country, anyway.’
Sheridan wrinkles her nose. ‘I hated watching that show. I had to stop in the end.’
‘I don’t believe I mentioned a show,’ Quinn says, mischievously.

‘They were doing everything wrong,’ Sheridan says. A nerve has been touched. She paces irately. ‘They didn’t have enough people. They didn’t trust each other. They didn’t spend nearly enough time on paperwork. Paperwork saves lives. But most of all I hated the will-they-or-won’t-they. It went on for years. It was forced, it was farcical.’ She glares at Quinn. ‘It doesn’t take that long to know. You will or you won’t. And then you do.’
Quinn reads her expression carefully. ‘You do?’
‘Yeah,’ Sheridan says, smiling again. ‘Yeah, I think you do.’
A distant rapid thudding noise slowly becomes apparent. Sheridan sees the source of the sound first and points. ‘Backup’s here. And it looks like we rated a helicopter after all.’
‘“They subsequently became romantically involved,”’ she mutters to herself.

Adam Quinn’s biography is contentless beyond this point. His life spent touring, playing, lecturing – and occasionally conducting, writing and composing – is documented in exhaustive, pointless detail. He withstands background checks and surveillance, and consistently demonstrates himself to represent zero risk of leak. He eventually receives the extremely low clearance level normally granted to long-term Organisation-external partners of Organisation staff.
They get married, and she takes his name, which she, reading, considers faintly unrealistic.

Sometime later, they try for a child, and fail. This page of the report, she stares at for a long time, feeling an eerie numbness spread through her. A numbness that should be something else, anything else. She should feel something, reading something like this, shouldn’t she? The topic of children is not mentioned anywhere else in the report.

Up until the end of the third round of questioning, Adam Quinn assumes good faith. He figures the repetition is a due diligence tic, a corporate procedural requirement. It’s only when they start over from ‘What’s your name?’ with a brand-new interviewer for the fourth time that he finally gets it: they don’t like him, and they don’t care what he thinks his name is. They’re trying to grind him down until he can’t think, until he’s just dust particles they can sift through for data.

He reacts badly to this realisation. He asks for his wife, and asks for his wife, and they ignore him, and they ignore him, and she persistently fails to appear, until it becomes a cold form of torture. The questions keep coming and nothing stops them, not answering truthfully, not not answering, not lying, not rambling off on tangents. They don’t stop until he begins falling asleep in the middle of his own sentences.

He wakes up in a standard Humanoid Containment Unit, a stackable one-bedroom apartment with holographic fake windows, impregnable walls and extensive discreet modifications for the security and monitoring of Unknown entities. This one is on the first basement level, but he can’t tell that. The bright ‘light’ pouring in through the main living area window is authentic enough to tan.

He wakes up on the couch with a start, feeling creaky and dehydrated. He realises that he slept in his suit, and that his suit is creased. He hates that, that sensation of not looking his best, or at least presentable. That’s going to gnaw away at him until he can find, at minimum, a razor and a change of shirt.

What woke him was the heavy metallic clack of the door unlocking. He looks up, rubbing his eyes. It’s his wife. ‘Marie! Oh, my God.’ He leaps up and rushes over to meet her. She stops him a few paces short, with a gesture and a cold smile. And that hurts. It hurts more than anything.

So it really happened: U-4987, Sunshine, has bitten out the part of Marie that cared about him. She wasn’t absent because of some unrelated ε-level breach. She just chose to be elsewhere, indifferent.
So he doesn’t embrace her. He stands at a polite distance. ‘How are you feeling? Did you sleep?’
‘I’m fine.’

‘I can tell you’ve had your coffee. Have you eaten? Come on, I’ll make you something.’ The unit has a rudimentary kitchen area. He goes through and starts exploring the cupboards. ‘There must be something edible around here. Eggs and milk, at least. I’m ashamed to say I more or less fell asleep where I was standing when they put me in here, so I haven’t had a chance to scout. Or do you keep the place empty, and the food arrives through a slot in the wall?’
Marie begins, ‘Mr Quinn –’
Adam shoots her an unhappy look.
‘Okay,’ she says, ‘Adam. Please come and sit down. You’re right, there’s nothing in any of those cupboards.’

He closes the cupboard and sits opposite her at the kitchen table. ‘Scrambled eggs on granary toast,’ he suggests. ‘With chives in the eggs. And cheese. That’s what we both need right now. Particularly you, because if I don’t make something substantial for you, you end up skipping meals outright. Or drinking those wretched wallpaper-paste milkshakes.’
‘Adam, we’ve been married for seventeen years, is that correct?’
‘Yes.’
‘I don’t know you.’

‘That’s fine,’ Adam says. ‘I doubt that that’s going to be a serious problem. You’ve told me, many times, about your own people who’ve lost themselves in the work and had to bootstrap their own personalities. You love watching it. It’s like watching butterflies emerge from chrysalides. The best of your people can turn that around in two weeks. Imagine how fast it’s going to be for you.’
‘No,’ Quinn replies. Her tone is clinical, matter-of-fact. ‘I’m afraid it’s not possible.’
‘What’s not possible?’

‘I can’t begin a new relationship right now. Certainly not something as serious as a marriage. You have nominal clearance; you know the broad strokes of what we do. I have responsibilities. I do not have … “time.”’
‘This isn’t “new,”’ Adam says, deadpan. ‘It’s preexisting.’
‘No,’ Quinn explains. ‘That relationship is ended now, and we are somewhere else.’
Adam stares at her for a long moment, thin-lipped. He asks her:
‘What do you remember?’
The question is so open-ended that Quinn doesn’t manage to respond verbally. She spreads her hands slightly, the gesture saying, ‘What?’

‘You don’t remember me,’ Adam says. ‘Sunshine also clearly ate the part of you that would care if you forgot me. As well as the part of you that cares about brunch. “What else have you forgotten?” would be a stupid question to ask, so instead I’m asking you, what’s left? I want you to tell me everything you can remember.’
‘Everything I can remember?’
‘Yes. Let’s say, from the last twenty years.’

It’s still a farcical question at face value, and Quinn’s first instinct is to dismiss it as such, but she thinks again. She thinks, intending to genuinely try to answer the question. And she finds gaps. There’s a dearth of specifics. It’s like being asked to ‘say something’ and immediately forgetting all words.
She says, ‘I remember … working.’

And driving home, and sleeping, and driving back to work. Big, hostile buildings. Drug regimens, containment protocols, endless piles of opaque numbers, personal fitness drills. Running. Calculating. Never, ever stopping calculating. She remembers, with unfair clarity, a variety of extremely bad dreams.
And other than that, nothing. A huge, deep, ragged-edged black pit.
Adam says, ‘You remember nothing good, do you? Nothing good at all.

‘When you come home, on the nights you make it home, you are ready to fold up. It has never been an easy job, but these past few years have been the worst they’ve ever been, because you’re coming to the conclusion of something gigantic. You have explained to me how it is that you can never tell me, really, what it is you do, without the act of you telling me killing me. And I – I couldn’t stand that at first, and I still hate your job and I think it’s a monstrous farce – but I trusted you in that. And I stopped asking. But I can tell, from the … rattle in your hands and the things you don’t say, and the way you sleep, that there is some kind of war going on back here. And you’re losing people to it. And you’re almost at the end. And you’re going to win.

‘So I scramble your eggs, and I play the violin for you, and between us we hack out about three-tenths of what I would consider to be normalcy. Not because you can’t do this without me, you could take the whole universe by yourself if you really had to, but to blazes with that, you shouldn’t have to.

‘We didn’t connect instantly. But we connected pretty damned fast. We had music in common at first, Bach and Mendelssohn and Nina Simone. We had tobacco in common and a mutual hatred of The X-Files. Then it was coffee and wine. And then after some time it became hiking, and birdwatching, and Perseid meteors. I don’t like to climb but I hold the rope when you climb. We like Bruce Lee flicks. We watch Poirot and Have I Got News for You and we read stacks and stacks of books … No, in fairness, it’s mainly me for the books. You don’t have the long-term time to spare anymore.’
He pinches the bridge of his nose for a second. Any two people can find that much common ground. Just being in the same place for years doesn’t count for anything. What do they have?

‘We communicate,’ he says. ‘Better than anybody I’ve seen. We can be apart for two months while I’m on tour or you’re overseas and snap right back and pick up a conversation from the word we left off. We’re connected, Marie. We are of a mind. You’ll see it all. It’ll happen again, just as fast. You’ve just got to give it a chance.’

Quinn is almost there. She sees the shape of what Adam is describing. It’s distant and unclear, but if she concentrates she might be able to bring it into focus. It worries her, for nebulous reasons she can’t completely articulate, but she can almost understand how there could be room for it. How it could lock into her life as it currently exists, and still make sense.

But Adam just said something crucial. He said a keyword that means the marriage counselling session is over and this is now a situation. Quinn can’t ignore it. She forces herself to drop the other thread and seize this one.
‘What war?’
And now Adam really doesn’t know what’s happening. ‘Good God. The war, Marie. I don’t know how else to describe it.’
‘What war? How many people?’

‘I don’t know,’ Adam says. ‘There are names. Names you stop mentioning, and then you ignore me when I bring them up again. I assume there are reasons. I don’t know the specifics. How could I know? Why don’t you know?’

Quinn races through the reasoning. The existence of a war computes. It confirms long-term suspicions. It could have been going on for years without her realising it. It makes sense to her that she could be fighting it – winning, even – and not know; pruning her own memories or losing them in skirmishes. This certainly won’t be the first time she’s uncovered it. It makes sense that Adam, naturally gifted with the mental equivalent of a thick layer of blubber, could stand on the edge of the conflict and dimly be able to perceive it. And the Division – so understaffed.
People are disappearing around her.
‘And what if –’ she begins, and stops dead in the middle of the thought, as if the thought itself was stolen out of her.

‘And what if we get back together, and –’ she begins again, and this time hard instinct seizes her around the midsection and bodily hauls her back from thinking a thought that, it knows, would kill her. She’s Wile E. Coyote, she’s already run off the edge of a precipice into clear air, and thinking that thought would be like looking down.
She feels U-4987 moving around her, abstractly bound to her, a winking speck of glitter in her eye. ‘Something isn’t right.’
Adam scratches at his own eye. ‘Do you see that?’
‘How can you see that?’

‘I have a mild immunity to antimemetic influence,’ Adam says. He knows it’s in his file and he knows Quinn has read the file, but apparently it needs to be said again. ‘I can tell when something is fritzing with my memories. I can resist it. Up to a point. So, Marie, I was hoping to have a relaxed conversation over coffee and get around to this topic organically, but I’m going to have to skip to the end: I have the impression that Sunshine is trying to kill me.’

‘… No,’ Quinn says. ‘That’s not its behaviour model. It doesn’t sustain itself that way, by eating people. It eats memories. And it’s never done this. Not to you, nor me, nor anybody. Not since the very early days. It’s tame, domesticated. It does exactly what I tell it to do. Even when I’m waiting, and I’m bored, and I let it eat my short-term, it sits and waits to be told to eat.’

‘Then what is it doing to us?’ Adam is getting nervy, and won’t let go of his eye. He stands up and backs away. ‘I would like it if we could figure this out quickly. We don’t have a way to put Sunshine down.’
There’s a sound in Quinn’s mind, but not in her ear, like a distant chorus of baying dogs. She stands too, and moves after Adam into the middle of the containment unit.
She says, ‘It’s trying to protect you.’
‘I – How does wiping your memory of me protect me?’
‘I can’t explain,’ Quinn says. ‘And I can’t explain why I can’t explain. I don’t fully know myself. There’s an X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​.’
‘A what?’
‘You can’t be here,’ she says. ‘You can’t be in my life. You have to leave, or you’re going to die.’

‘I’m not leaving you,’ Adam says. ‘Christ, that’s why we did it in the end. Tied the knot, I mean. It was scintillatingly obvious to both of us, very early on, that we were forever. But I wanted to get it on the public record. I stood up in front of everybody I respect and I swore to them that I would protect you.’
U-4987 is agitated. Quinn feels it flitting around the room, incoherent, trying to tell her what it needs.
She says, with sudden clarity, ‘I must have made the same promise.’

Adam doubles over, blinded in both eyes now. Closing his eyes does nothing, covering his eyes does nothing. The gold-white light is strobing for him, moving into violet. He panics. ‘Help. Help me. I can’t see.’ He reaches out, unsteadily, for Quinn’s hand. She lets him take it and pull her close. The light doesn’t fade. He clings to Quinn for a few moments, and she holds on to him until he realises that U-4987 is completely within her control, and this is all intentional.
‘You’re going to do this?’ Adam says. ‘This is the UO mandate, this is what “keeping everyone safe” amounts to? You’ve got no idea what you’re about to do to yourself. You don’t even know me.’
‘I think I know,’ she replies.
‘You will feel this for the rest of your life. Every day, you will wake up with a sick cold feeling in your stomach where there used to be a real life. And you’ll wonder why.’

Unseen by him, Quinn takes the autoinjector out of her pocket and regards it for a second. She reaches a conclusion. Determination flashes in her eyes. ‘I’m going to win this war,’ she says to him. ‘I’ll beat the universe. And then I will come and find out why.’

Adam holds on to her for another long, long moment. He can hear the baying too, now, and he can even barely perceive what it is, far off behind the hill, that Sunshine is frantic about. That distant dot, that fleeting secondhand glimpse of the shape of it, far off, is enough to terrify him.

He has faith. He knows how fast Marie can put the jigsaw pieces back together, work against a world that makes no sense to her, isolate the truth. He knows she can take the universe. But a sharp misgiving jabs him in the stomach and he can’t stop himself saying: ‘And what if you lose?’
She kisses him. It’s a stranger’s kiss; there’s nothing there Adam recognises. He breaks off, unsettled. It’s a whisper now: ‘What if you lose?’
Quinn exits the containment unit; she slams and deadlocks the door with a single movement. The heavy metallic crack makes the whole building shake.
There are people outside. Ives, Jamie Sitt, Simon Lee and a few others, comparing notes. They look appalled.
She still has the injector in one hand. She holds it to one side, aimed downward, like a weapon. It is capped and unused.

‘Fill in his backstory,’ she tells them. ‘He was never married. Relocate him to where I’ll never find him, incinerate all the evidence, then report to me for surgical memory erasure. I’ll do myself last.’
And she slots the injector back into her inner jacket pocket.
Ives looks as if he has an objection. She stares him down.
‘My husband’s dead,’ she says.

chapters.title: 3: Case Colorless Green
paragraphs: Case Colorless Green
‘Simon, do you have a minute?’

Lee’s expression freezes. It is nine months since he tangled with Adrian Gage/U-7175, he is all the way caught up, and he has relearned and re-re-relearned that ‘a minute’ is Quinn-speak for ‘an hour, maybe the rest of the day.’
‘Sure,’ he lies.

Quinn hands a thin folder to him. A U-document. In modern times, the Unknown database sprawls across multiple storage formats: solid state, tape, paper files. Quinn likes paper. It has some valuable attributes. It’s a shade harder to lose, for one. ‘Something in my reminders went ping just now. It was about this.’
He opens the seal. There is just one sheet of paper inside.
Ref.: U-3125 • Cat.: ε0

Containment protocol: U-3125 is kept at the UO Wyeleigh Passive Containment Archive, level 1, vault 601. This containment unit is a 10 × 15 × 3m cuboidal room clad in layers of lead, soundproofing and telepathic shielding. Access is through an airlock system at one end of the containment unit. This airlock is programmed to allow only one person to enter the containment unit at a time, and to remain locked until this person exits before allowing another person to enter.

Under no circumstances may any coherent information be allowed to leave the containment unit. This includes written and electronic notes, photographs, audio and video recordings, sound, electromagnetic and particle-based signals, and psi emanations. During the exit cycle, a purge system rigged to the airlock flushes the occupant’s memory by flooding the airlock with amnestic gas for three minutes.
A senior Antimemetics Division staff member must visit U-3125 every 63 days.
END OF FILE
He verifies that the other side is blank. ‘You’re kidding me. That’s the whole entry?’
‘That’s the whole entry,’ Quinn says.

It isn’t even the fiftieth strangest thing Lee’s seen in the files, but still: ‘No description, no acquisition report, no test log, no addenda? No clue who built the unit, or when, or how many times it’s been visited, or who carried out the previous visits, or what they took in with them, or how long they were in there?’
‘Come with me.’
He gestures at his laptop. ‘I’m preparing education. Ideatic measure spaces. These newcomers are quick at the math, I can’t stay out ahead of them.’
Quinn flicks her head in the direction they need to go. He sighs and brings the machine with him.

It’s a five-minute walk from Lee’s desk to the room where the containment unit is installed. One question answers itself immediately: the vault is an Ed Hix model. His signature style of containment architecture is recognisable a mile out. Sleek, white, plainly impregnable without the aid of extremely heavy tools. Hix died under anomalously unclear circumstances more than a decade ago, which means the unit is at least that old.
Neither Lee nor Quinn recognises it.
‘Ten years is sixty-odd visits. Or more,’ Quinn says. ‘As for the rest of the omissions, I expect there are good reasons.’

‘You expect,’ Lee echoes, doubtfully. ‘I don’t like the idea of you routinely exposing yourself to an Unknown so dangerous that we can’t even write the reason why we can’t write it down down. Especially because it’s impossible for us to recover any usable information this way. You’re going to go in, be incommunicado for two hours, and come out a smiling amnesiac. What do we gain from that? It’s just a breach risk.’
